reflection.theme: The Life You Couldn't See From Down There
reflection.quote: The world breaks everyone, and afterward many are strong at the broken places.
reflection.reading:
When I was 22, I thought sobriety was a sentence. A smaller room, a quieter life, a long list of things I'd never get to do again. What I couldn't see — what no one drunk can really see — is that the room I was living in was already small. I just couldn't measure it because I never left.

It's June. The days are long and the evenings stretch out like an invitation. For a lot of people, that means patios and weddings and bonfires, and a quiet panic about how to be there sober. I get it. But here's what I wish someone had told me earlier: the life on the other side of the drink isn't a consolation prize. It's the actual thing. It's standing on top of a mountain in February with your legs shaking from a real run, not a hangover. It's remembering the conversation. It's being someone your friends call when something hard happens, because they know you'll pick up.

Hemingway wasn't writing about sobriety, but he understood something about the geography of being broken. The cracks are where the strength sets in. You don't get to skip them. But you do get to decide what you build on top of them — and from down there, you can't see how far the view goes.
reflection.meditation: What does the version of me five years from now want me to do today?

affirmations: I am building a life I don't need to escape from.
Today I show up for the small, ordinary things — they're what the good life is made of.
I am capable of being present, even when presence is uncomfortable.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful today for a clear head on a long summer evening. For friends who know my real name and not just my bar order. For the unspectacular gift of waking up without dread, and for the eleven years of mornings that taught me what that's worth.
gratitude.closing: One more day. That's the whole deal.
